item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks, Chris!

Haven't been signing in for ages, but I owed you those thanks… ;>
(not sure 'thanks' is actually plural, but you get my meaning!)

item.title: I made a couple of changes
item.content:encoded:
• The Foundation now addresses ██████ ██████ as Doctor (if you know who this is meant to be, you'll get why) however Larry still calls him Mr cause he's an idiot.
  • I've added a crosslink to a wonderful tale based on this SCP written by Eta Carinae some time ago. To be honest I'm not sure why it's taken me so long to do this.

I may make some changes to other parts of this SCP but for now this is all that's changed.

item.title: Re: Question
item.content:encoded: If you've ever seen Demo N' Rappy, it's like Rappy (and was actually part of the inspiration for the raptor)

item.title: Question
item.content:encoded: Does SCP-2699-4 walk as a raptor normally would, or does it attempt to walk on all fours like a housecat?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good point, changing it now

Edit: Think I got my Sapient and Sentient definitions round the wrong way

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the elephant, that one's actually an ongoing discussion. They're at least chimp smart, with some things suggesting borderline people-smart (well documented death rituals and altruistic acts which inconvenience the elephant, for instance).

The others are more sentient than sapient, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2699-2's intelligence is considerably lower than that of an average human; it seems to have a basic understanding of its surroundings but is not capable of communication or solving simple logic puzzles such as opening doors.

Not sapient

SCP-2699-3's behaviour patterns are typical of a juvenile African elephant

Baby elephants don't tend to be sapient, do they?

Its behaviour is similar to that of a domestic house cat

Neither are cats.

And if the skunk's sapient, why doesn't the Foundation interwiew it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: [DUPLICATE POST PLEASE IGNORE]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not sure what you mean by them not being sapient?

The luminescence isn't really the animal doing it, in my head it's more they just painted it with glow in the dark paint.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the idea of bootleg animals, and the Ashens reference was great, however there were some minor issues:

SCP-2699 is a collection of 4 sapient creatures

The animals are not sapient

luminescent

Shouldn't this be bioluminescent?

Anyway, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reptileasaur©! Fun ages for 3-12! Direction: Feeding twice and plam. Do not the danger!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah I can understand this, I did try and come up with ways around it but to get the desired effect I wanted I had to leave that in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Knock off Mammals? I'll have you know Clever Raptor is a 100% authentic Reptileasaur.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What in the fuck. Fucking knock-off mammals? How did I miss this before? I can't not upvote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm no-voting because it irked me how much things were spelled out in the list of defects and were sometimes repeated from the Description.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just read this so I can evaluate a tale draft. While I really dig the concept, there are some tonal issues that prevent me from upvoting:

+ show block
– hide block


Firstly the article makes extensive use of imprecise terms of orientation like "front", "back" and "rear" rather than the terminology of biology like "anterior", "dorsal", and "posterior".

the usual unpleasant smell that skunks are known for.

"the typical spray" is probably sufficient, although "the thiol-based compounds normally found in skunk spray" is more precise. The current phrase reads much like it is in Randall Monroe's Thing Explainer.

similar in appearance to Stone Age tools (specifically stone age axes).

The field of paleathropology has an extensive vocabulary for describing stone tools and "similar to Stone Age tools" is far too vague. Similar Neolithic or Paleolithic tools? Similar to flint tools, chert tools, obsidian tools? What core type? Which knapping type?

similar in appearance to a South American male human.

What does this mean? Everybody south of Panama doesn't look the same, and there are South Americans of basically every ethnic group on Earth (including origins in Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, South Asia, East Asia, and lots more). I assumed this meant an indigenous South American (which even so has distinct ethnicities) but later it apparently was intended to be Latino.

simple logic puzzles such as opening doors

I don't think opening doors is technically a logic puzzle. Logic puzzles involve connecting information and eliminating impossible answers. Also psychology has methods of quantifying developmental intelligence, and describing deviations from nominal adult cognition.

There's more, but all of it is similar, there are correct terms for things, and this article should use them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just want to add that I love the containment procedures here, because you're left utterly bewildered by the end of them. What is this, and why?

Also I really feel sorry for these creations, although Velocikitty seems to be mostly hale and hearty.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Me too. You can educate cats not to bite, and this runs the same software as a cat, so no problem. I guess the claws aren't retractable, but we can always put plastic safety caps on their tips. Then i'll have a velocikitteh to snuggle with! ^_^

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't care if it's a cheap knockoff or inherently dangerous, I want the "Clever Raptor." Like, I want one in a bad way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's a bit overly wordy in places but still a solid +1. Pretty unique.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is indeed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just checking, this is inspired by Stuart Ashen's videos, right?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oops yeah, changing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You still forgot to add the -2 in the second one. That is, if I'm getting this right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2699-2 was originally found in a Mexican style poncho, large sombrero hat and a fake moustache (all of which are contained as SCP-2699-2-a) that had been stuck to its face and maracas which had been glued via unknown means to its hands.

SCP-2699-A were removed from SCP-2699-2 successfully without causing damage to either it or SCP-2699-2.

Small discrepancy here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know, I still do believe that it'd be more fun if you have the letter being written by Aslan - it's already a wacked-out premise, I don't think you risk much by pushing the wackiness to its highest level. Also, the idea of a letter full of paw-mark doodles and "sorry about the spelling but last time I was a Lamb so now I'm a beeping Lion instead" amuses me.

(Also, if you can take the neat, concise dot-point format of the list at the end and adapt it into the Description, that'd help the flow a lot ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like how it was written. Is this Intern working for God? Neat-o. I didn't know God has a company.

I'm assuming China, or an entity resembling China made them. Right?

EDIT: Them being the SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is riddled with execution issues.

+ line-by-line
- hide text

Due to the nature of SCP-2699, each instance requires different containment protocols.

This is a filler sentence, as you then go on to lay out different containment protocols.

SCP-2699-1's front paws are to be checked every 24 hours for any wounds or infections, and if any are found they must be treated immediately to prevent further damage.

This sentence strikes me as being kind of clunky. You could streamline it by saying "SCP-2699-1 is to be checked every 24 hours and treated immediately in the case of wounds or infections in its front paws."

SCP-2699-1-a is to remain in archaeological storage unless required for analysis. Care is to be taken when removing this object from storage as it is very fragile.

This isn't so much an execution problem particular to you as it is a sitewide practice that I wouldn't mind seeing cleaned up. But I don't know that it's necessary to give the skunk's poking rock its own subclassification. That seems almost humorously over-complex to me. Also, "as it is very fragile" sounds informal. "Due to its fragility, object is to be handled with care" would be an improvement, but it still doesn't really seem necessary to me.

SCP-2699-2 must be contained in a padded cell. SCP-2699-2 is to be placed in a harness, allowing it to stand upright but not allowing it to move around its cell. To prevent muscle atrophy, at least 2 personnel are to take it out of its harness and move it around its cell twice a day for at least 5 minutes.

This is getting kind of overly complex. I know a lot of people say they skip over containment procedures entirely, so this may seem overly nitpicky, but if it's going to be here, it should be as concise and readable as possible. I'm not sure you need to go to the trouble of the specific exercise procedures (and I don't think that a total of 10 minutes of exercise a day is going to help anything), when you could probably just say that staff are required to assist it with physical activity or exercise on a daily basis or whatever.

and is to be fed cow, pig or horse meat.

Oddly specific. Couldn't you just say "a diet of meat" or something, or is there a reason it can't be fed goats, ocelots, gerbils, chickens, etc.?

however they appear to be heavily mutated.

Someone with a more thorough background in biology may want to chime in here, but this is pinging my "dubious use of a scientific term" senses.

Another noticeable difference is that

This lead-in to the sentence has a very "school essay" quality to it, and seems kind of odd considering the sentence before states that the most notable difference is already the differing stripe pattern. I think it might flow better if you just said something along the lines of "SCP-2699-1 also lacks a tail".

(at first it was believed that the tail had been removed but further analysis confirms that a tail was never present)

I feel like some sort of transition between "removed" and "but" is needed to prevent this from sounding like a run-on sentence.

The anal scent glands of this specimen seem to give off a smell that is recognisable as lavender

"Seem to"? It does or it doesn't. Especially given that the smell is apparently specifically recognizable.

rather than the usual unpleasant smell that skunks are known for

"That skunks are known for" is unscientific tone. Also that's somewhat redundant combined with the "usual" descriptor.

When SCP-2699-1 was discovered, a stone tool (SCP-2699-1-a) was in its right paw which, after analysis, has been confirmed to look be similar in appearance to other Stone Age tools (specifically stone age axes).

The way the sentence is written implies that they tested the skunk's right paw and not the tool. I'm also pretty sure that they wouldn't be testing to see if it was "similar in appearance" to other Stone Age tools, but rather would testing it to see if it was similar in composition or artifice or something. Also, is "Stone Age tool" an accurate archeological descriptor?

SCP-2699-2 is a humanoid creature similar in appearance to a South-American male human

"South-American", beyond being improperly hyphenated, is not anything close to a meaningful ethnic descriptor, considering you have an asston of different ethnicities in South America. I'm assuming that since he's an oh-so-hilarious Mexican stereotype, that he would be at the very least an Hispanic male human, or whatever.

It does, however, considerably increase the chance of SCP-2699-2 catching infections

I don't think "catching" an infection is the preferred term in a scientific document.

SCP-2699-2 has no knee or elbow joints and, after X-ray analysis, it was revealed

Inefficient use of language here. "X-ray analysis has shown that SCP-2699-2 has no etc.".

requiring help to stand upright again

I think "assistance" would sound more clinical than "help" here.

SCP-2699-2's intelligence is considerably lower than that of an average human; it seems to have a basic understanding of its surroundings but is not capable of communication or solving simple logic puzzles such as opening doors.

I'm not quite sure how they would make that determination if he's incapable of communication. Is he unintelligent, or is he brain-damaged or something? It seems like a distinction that researchers probably would have looked into.

SCP-2699-2 was originally found in a Mexican style poncho, large sombrero hat and a fake moustache (all of which are contained as SCP-2699-2-a) that had been stuck to its face and maracas which had been glued via unknown means to its hands.

For Christ's sake. Really?

SCP-2699-3 is a creature similar in appearance to a juvenile African elephant; however, a few differences have been noticed.

The "a few differences have been noticed" is another example of the "school essay" thing you've got going here. I would just go into how it's different rather than announcing that it's different and here's why it's different.

SCP-2699-3's behaviour patterns are typical of a juvenile African elephant and can become increasingly stressed when in containment. It was decided that SCP-2699-3 would be allowed some minor freedom on Foundation-controlled grounds outside of containment to relieve its stress.

"Stressed" is a vague descriptor, to the point of meaninglessness. Was it depressed? Violent? Lethargic? Some combination thereof, or something else?

SCP-2699-4 is an unidentified species of reptile resembling Velociraptor mongoliensis as depicted in the popular movie "Jurassic Park".

If it's just a normal velociraptor except bigger, wouldn't you just describe it that way? I know the Note at the End makes that comparison, but I don't see why scientists would.

Its behaviour is similar to that of a domestic house cat; It is very friendly to anyone who enters its containment cell

"Friendly" is not a scientific term at all, and if my experience with cats holds any weight, I don't think they act in such a uniform manner from specimen to specimen that this is at all a meaningful description.

It has been discovered after a minor power cut in Building B5 of Site-131 that SCP-2699-4 becomes luminescent under low light conditions.

What's a "minor power cut"? Seems to me the power either goes out or it doesn't. Also, unless they keep this thing in a lighted enclosure 24/7, wouldn't they have observed this earlier?

It is unknown if the container itself is anomalous, as it is not known how it arrived at its discovery location (see addendum), and until further analysis can confirm its anomalous status it is to remain in secure storage.

This comes across as filler. I'm not really certain whether or not we need to know if the container that these things showed up in is anomalous or not.

I don't really like the core idea for this article much. It's implied that the dude writing the letter is an intern for either Abrahamic God or an Abrahamic God-like entity. So if that's the case, why is God sending counterfeit products to some goober on YouTube for review so that it can take "legal action"? Wouldn't it just God-magic some solution to its problem immediately? I dunno. Any possible sense behind this seems to have been ignored in the name of lolsogoofy; that works for enough of the readership for this to be positively rated, I guess, but I think that's kind of an overrepresented genre of skips in Series 3.

I'm not a huge fan of the racist Mexican caricature either. Like, I get that it's the counterfeiter in the story doing it, but it doesn't even really make internal sense with the rest of the anomaly. The counterfeiter doesn't understand enough about animals to know that skunks aren't sapient tool-users or that elephants aren't amphibians, but it's savvy enough about human culture to portray a Mexican dude in a stereotypical manner? I get that racism exists in the world and that it is sometimes necessary to tell a story, but it's one of those things that you'd better have a good reason for and you'd better do well, and neither condition is really met here.

Also, you describe the animals as being sapient, but you don't really go anywhere with it. Especially with the frogaphant. You mention that it's "stressed". That could actually be kind of interesting. What does a frog-elephant abomination think, anyway? But you do absolutely nothing with that element of the story after introducing it, which renders it clutter in the context of the piece.

Really, though, the conceptual issues ultimately take a back seat to the execution. For the reasons outlined in the line-by-line, I don't really view the writing here as up to mainlist standards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually just came here from binge-watching ashens videos, so this struck me as pretty funny—once I got to the part about the humanoid only having skin on one side, I had an idea of what was coming. I love the concept.
The sentence flow can occasionally be a little choppy, but it looks like you've done a good job editing the grammar: I didn't notice any obvious mistakes in my read-through.
+1 for knock-off organisms.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Out of curiosity, is the YouTube reviewer supposed to be a reference to Stuart Ashen?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I believe Crayne did the tags but I dunno how the tags work exactly so maybe it is an error, I put it as safe as they're not particularly hard to look after and they're not particularly dangerous. SCP-2699-2 is probably the most difficult to look after but still isn't that difficult.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The object class is listed as safe, but it's been tagged with Euclid. Is that an error? I was reading this thinking "how is this safe" when it's several living things with individual needs and containment procedures.

Other than that, I found the references to Jurassic Park in the description was a bit of an immersion breaker for me. I get why it's there, in that the incompetent creator of these objects has used JP as a source for the raptor, but having it mentioned in the recovered letter seems enough to illustrate this to me. That whole section (2699-4) felt wonky to me.

Overall, I like the concept enough to not downvote, but there's some stuff in there that doesn't read right to me at the moment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll try and do my best to fix the tone and grammar errors (haven't done English since high school so my grammar is crappy)

Edit: hopefully the grammar is a bit better now

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, the idea is probably quite good, but literally just the typographical and mechanical errors (needs commas in some places, while some of the commas that are there need to be semicolons) and lack of clinical tone kill the document for me. I gotta downvote until it's edited and adjusted to be more in line with general expectations of a Foundation document.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea is good, but the clinical tone is quite dead at some points. Also, the way the Jurasic Park movie is refered seems quite odd for a Foundation document.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First off I have to say that you had immediately had my +1 when you put the Latin names for the animals you had included.

But disregarding that, the SCP in itself is very well done and the containment procedures were reasonable as was the classification (There wasn't any troubles with any of the SCP incidents apart from 2699-4 which only did minor injuries).

All in all, this is a really good SCP and I hope you continue to make ones that are on par with this one, always strive for better!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First off, the execution's mostly great, though I find some of the stuff a bit padded in regards to the not-elephant's skin. It doesn't bother me too much though, so I can shrug it off.

As for the idea presented here, I quite like it. You've captured the weird and humourous aspects of knock-off toys darn well alongside the creepy implications just lurking there, not to mention the mysterious origins make it all more ominous.

Wondertainment's got some stiff competition here.

item.title: Here it is
item.content:encoded:
Ok so I have been working on this for a few weeks. Decided it's time to grit my teeth and bare it as I get reviewed by the wiki.

Thanks to all who have helped, those who I remember that have helped are: Kaktus, Athena_grey, Paroxsym, Nanoro, Ornstein

So here we go, let's see how it goes.
